coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid-assisted preparation of N, S-rich carbon dots as efficient corrosion inhibitors
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tianxiang
Cao, Shuyun
Sun, Youqing
Liu, Dan
Pan, Shiguang
Ma, Aijing
Kuvarega, Alex T.
Mamba, Bhekie B.
Gui, Jianzhou
coredata.dc:description: A series of (N, S-rich) carbon dots were prepared from L-cysteine via the typical solvothermal method with 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide as the solvent by varying synthesis time. The experimental results indicated that the carbon dots synthesized for 60 min, the biggest in average carbon dot sizes (about 117.0 nm), highest pyrrole-like N contents (62.9%), showed the best corrosion inhibition performance with a high efficiency of 97% at 40 mg/L of inhibitors for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, which was ascribed to the fact that the adsorption of the carbon dots followed the Langmuir adsorption model. Correspondingly, a potential corrosion inhibition mechanism was proposed by surface characterization results, and the parallel adsorption mode of pyrrole-like N species on steel surface by π-complex formation contributed dominantly to the excellent inhibition performance. The work provided a general solvothermal method for carbon dots inhibitor synthesis assisted by ionic liquids, and the structure-inhibition relationship gave the guideline for the theoretical design of corrosion inhibitors.

XPS spectra of the four IL-CDs: (a) S 2p spectra, (b) N 1 s spectra.

Nyquist diagram for the carbon steel corroded in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution uninhibited or inhibited with IL-CDs/CDs (a1) and with different concentrations of IL-CDs3 (b1); Bode plot for the carbon steel corroded in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution inhibited with IL-CDs/CDs (a2) and with different concentrations of IL-CDs3 (b2).
Electrochemical equivalent circuit for fitting the impedance spectra for steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with and without IL-CDs.
Potentiodynamic polarization plots for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution uninhibited and inhibited with different concentrations of the IL-CDs synthesized at different reaction times.

Nyquist plots of carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing 40 mg/L IL-CDs3 when varying exposure time.

The Langmuir adsorption plot for the steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution in the presence of (a) IL-CDs1, (b) IL-CDs2, (c) IL-CDs3, (d) IL-CDs4 with different concentrations.
The XPS spectra of the steel surface in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution after 2 h of immersion inhibited with 40 mg/L IL-CDs3: (a) C 1 s; (b) N 1 s; (c) S 2p; (d) O 1 s.

Electrochemical parameters obtained from EIS for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with and without IL-CDs3 at 298 K.

Electrochemical parameters obtained from EIS for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with 40 mg/L IL-CDs3 at different temperatures.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of (N, S-rich) carbon dots were prepared from L-cysteine via the typical solvothermal method with 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide as the solvent by varying synthesis time. The experimental results indicated that the carbon dots synthesized for 60 min, the biggest in average carbon dot sizes (about 117.0 nm), highest pyrrole-like N contents (62.9%), showed the best corrosion inhibition performance with a high efficiency of 97% at 40 mg/L of inhibitors for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, which was ascribed to the fact that the adsorption of the carbon dots followed the Langmuir adsorption model. Correspondingly, a potential corrosion inhibition mechanism was proposed by surface characterization results, and the parallel adsorption mode of pyrrole-like N species on steel surface by π-complex formation contributed dominantly to the excellent inhibition performance. The work provided a general solvothermal method for carbon dots inhibitor synthesis assisted by ionic liquids, and the structure-inhibition relationship gave the guideline for the theoretical design of corrosion inhibitors.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Ionic liquid-assisted preparation of carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfuric acid is widely used in various applications, such as industrial pickling, acid cleaning, and acid descaling, which generally results in severe metallic corrosion [1,2]. Concerns are growing that acid attacks against metallic materials like iron would become a long-standing problem for metal protection in its practical industrial applications [3]. To resolve this challenging issue, the addition of inhibitors, as a simple, effective, and low-cost method, is introduced to retard corrosion towards metallic materials [4,5]. In general, the majority of available inhibitors successfully used in acidic media are organic compounds, which are mainly composed of organic heterocyclic rings or aromatic rings containing N, O, S, P as well as unsaturated bonds such as conjugated double and triple bonds [6,7]. The electronegative polar groups of the organic compounds, containing N, O, S, and P, can be firmly fixed onto the metal surface via chemisorption, while mutual interaction between the charged organic compound and the charged metal surface occurred by physical adsorption (mainly electrostatic interaction). In most cases, chemisorption and physical adsorption can work together to generate a protective film onto the metal surface, thus inhibiting the metal against corrosion [8,9]. Although lots of organic inhibitors can effectively inhibit the corrosion of metals, their excessive use would result in severe environmental pollution, posing a threat to human health [10]. As a consequence, it is of great necessity to develop an environmentally friendly, inexpensive, and facile preparation method for inhibitors.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of carbon materials, have attracted significant attention due to their excellent solubility in water, low toxicity, unique optical properties, good biocompatibility, simple preparation, low cost, etc. Now CDs have promising applications in many fields, such as biosensing, bioimaging, energy storage, catalysis, etc. [11-13]. In particular, the fluorescent properties, the electronic properties, and surface functional groups of CDs can be modulated by doping modification of CDs, which provides a possibility for its potential application in the field of corrosion inhibition [14,15]. More recently, CDs have already been reported to be applied in metal corrosion protection. Cui et al. [16] proposed that N-doped CDs can be utilized as ideal inhibitors for Fe in the acidic medium, with the best inhibition efficiency reaching 90%. Generally, as for the inhibition efficiency of an organic inhibitor, heteroatom follows the following order, O < N < S < P [17]. Therefore, it can be assumed that N, S-doped CDs tend to be effective inhibitors. Cen et al. [18] chose 4-aminosalicylic acid and thiourea as raw materials to prepare an N, S-doped CDs via conventional hydrothermal method as inhibitors for Fe corrosion in CO2-saturated 3.5% NaCl solution, and the best inhibition efficiency was 96.7%. At present, most of the CDs used as inhibitors are prepared by the hydrothermal/dissolution heat method [19]. However, there still exist some problems in the preparation of CDs, such as the aggregation and particle size uniformity [19], which may affect the inhibition performance of CDs. Ye et al. [20] prepared CDs as inhibitors for carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution by heating ammonium citrate at 180 °C for 1, 2, and 3 h, respectively, and the corresponding inhibition performance was 95.86%, 96.96%, and 86.50% at a CDs concentration of 200 mg/L, respectively. They noticed that the prepared CDs showed significant agglomeration at the reaction time of 3 h, leading to lower inhibition performance. The ease of agglomeration character of CDs limits their applications in corrosion inhibition; therefore, CDs preparation in different synthetic methods is challenging and necessary.

Ionic liquids (ILs), regarded as “green” materials, possess a great deal of superior physiochemical properties, including high solubility, low vapor pressure, excellent thermal stability, high ionic conductivity, low surface tension etc. [21]. Meanwhile, the properties of ILs can be designed by varying structural anions and cations [22]. Due to the low interfacial energy, ILs can prevent the aggregation of nanomaterials, and the low surface tension simultaneously facilitates the rapid nucleation of nanomaterials [23]. Currently, ILs have been used in the synthesis of CDs [24,25]. Wang et al. [26] obtained thermally stable and well-dispersed CDs by hydrothermal method with citric acid as the carbon source and ionic liquid (ILs), 1-aminopropyl-3-methyl-imidazolium bromide ([APMIm][Br]), as a capping agent. The interesting phenomenon of ILs attachment to form a capped shell, which improves the thermal stability of carbon dots and has an excellent anionic response, is expected to promote the application of CDs in various fields. Until now, the assistance of ILs for improving the dispersion of CDs in corrosion inhibition has not been reported in the literature.

In this work, IL-CDs have been successfully synthesized via a facile solvothermal strategy with cheap and environmentally friendly L-cysteine as raw materials and ionic liquid 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide [Bmim]Br as the solvent, respectively. The as-synthesized IL-CDs were then subjected to a series of characterizations, such as the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), photoluminescence (PL), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Besides, their inhibition performance was evaluated using the electrochemical method (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) against the corrosion of carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Finally, a possible corrosion inhibition mechanism was also proposed and elucidated according to the testing and analytical results from surface analytical techniques (scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and XPS).

1-butyl-3methylimidazolium bromide ([Bmim]Br, 99%) was purchased from Adamas-beta, L-cysteine (L-cys, 99%) was purchased from Shanghai Lanji, and sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) were purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan. They are all used as received. Carbon steel 45# (Cr, ≤0.2 wt%; Cu, ≤0.2 wt%; Ni, ≤0.3 wt%; Si, 0.2–0.4 wt%; C, 0.4–0.5 wt%; Mn, 0.5–0.8 wt%; and Fe, the rest) with the dimensions of 10 × 10 × 2 mm was used.

Shown in Fig. 1 was the schematic illustration of the preparation process of IL-CDs. In a typical procedure, L-cys (5 g) was mixed with [Bmim]Br (2 g) inside a beaker, under magnetic stirring, and then ultra-sonicating for 20 min. Subsequently, the mixed solution was placed into a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave with the heating temperature being set at 240 °C and kept for 15, 30, 60, and 120 min, respectively. After the reaction was over, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally, and the obtained solution was dispersed into 80 mL ionized water and sonicated for another 20 min. Afterward, the product was subjected to centrifugation at 8000 r/min for 15 min for separation. Further purification of the crude product was proceeded in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off 1000 Da) for 24 h, and the deionized water was changed every 6 h. After dialysis, the obtained solution was dried at 60 °C (in a vacuum drying oven) for four days. Finally, IL-CDs1 (reaction time: 15 min), IL-CDs2 (reaction time: 30 min), IL-CDs3 (reaction time: 60 min), and IL-CDs4 (reaction time: 120 min) of brownish-black solid were obtained. To compare corrosion inhibition performance of CDs prepared with or without the IL assistance, a blank sample, named CDs, was prepared by pyrolysis at 240 ℃ for 60 min without IL assistance.

FTIR (Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS50), UV–Vis (Thermo Scientific Evolution 300) and PL (F-380) tests of 1 g/L IL-CDs aqueous solution were performed. The chemical composition of the IL-CDs was evaluated by XPS (Thermo Fisher NEXSA). The morphology of the IL-CDs was measured using TEM (JEM-F200); before TEM tests, IL-CDs were dispersed in ethanol and sonicated for 1 h and then dropped onto the surface of copper mesh, and dried.

The test solutions were prepared from 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions without IL-CDs and with different concentrations of IL-CDs. The concentration range of four IL-CDs is 10, 20, 30, and 40 mg/L. The 0.5 M H2SO4 solution was prepared by diluting concentrated H2SO4 with deionized water.

The electrochemical tests were carried out via a traditional electrochemical workstation (Gamry, Reference 3000) in a three-electrode glass cell with an electrolyte volume of 200 mL. Usually, the carbon steel is the most widely used industrially, so a carbon steel sample with a 1 cm2 exposed surface (the rest surface was covered with epoxy resin) was employed as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl with fine luggin’s capillaries was used as the reference electrode and the platinum sheet as the counter electrode. Before each electrochemical test, the steel samples were mechanically polished using #180, #600, #1500, to #2000 wet SiC paper, then rinsed with deionized water and isopropyl alcohol, respectively. Finally, the product was dried with cold air for the electrochemical tests immediately. The well-polished electrode was first immersed in the test solution at 25 °C for 1 h under non-stirring conditions. Then the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test at the open circuit potential (Eocp ) was performed in the frequency range of 100 KHz ∼ 50 mHz with a sinusoidal AC current of 10 mV amplitude. Zsimpwin software was used to analyze the obtained EIS data. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) test was followed by the EIS test and performed at ± 250 mV versus Ag/AgCl electrode at 0.5 mV∙s−1. The corrosion potential and corrosion current density were collected using the Tafel extrapolation method and the repetetive experiments has been taken for electrochemical tests.

The surfaces of the carbon steel were firstly pretreated by polishment and cleaning as described above (in section 2.5). Then the steel samples were exposed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with/without 40 mg/L IL-CDs for two hours at 25 °C to observe the morphological changes before and after corrosion. After completion of immersion, the samples were dried with cold air and taken for surface examination, including SEM (Hitachi TM3030), AFM (BRUKER), XPS (Thermo Fisher NEXSA) measurements.

Shown in Fig. 2 (a) were the FTIR spectra of the IL-CDs. As for the four IL-CDs, the peaks at 3290, 2942, 1654, 1538, 1157, 1025 and 618 cm−1 were assigned as OH and NH, CH, CO, CC, NH2, C-N and C-S, respectively [27,28]. In particular, CO and CN are the characteristic functional groups of acylamide, which indicates that the product is a new product obtained by the dehydration condensation of cysteine. While N-H, C-N and C-S groups indicate the successful doping of N, S into IL-CDs. As can be seen from the UV–vis spectroscopy in Fig. 2(b), two distinct absorption bands at 200–219 and 300–400 nm corresponding to the absorption peaks of the leap of the CC bond in the aromatic ring in the carbon nucleus and the leap of the CO group, respectively [29]. As shown in Fig. S1(a, b, c, d), the maximum emission wavelengths of the four prepared IL-CDs, IL-CDs1, IL-CDs2, IL-CDs3 and IL-CDs4, were 458.6, 469.2, 497.8 and 498.3 nm, corresponding to excitation wavelengths of 380, 400, 420 and 440 nm, respectively. The fluorescence properties arise from the intrinsic state emission and the defective state emission, which correlate with the quantum size effect and the molecular state, respectively, and it is inconsistent with the previous report [30]. From the TEM images in Fig. 3 (100 carbon dots were counted), it could be obviously observed that the IL-CDs are uniformly distributed spherical nanoparticles, and the particle sizes of the IL-CDs prepared for different heating times of 15, 30, 60, and 120 min are 29.6, 60.1, 117.0, and 100.3 nm, respectively. Larger particle size than has been reported in other literature (about 10 nm) [14]. During the preparation process of IL-CDs, ionic liquids were not involved in the formation of carbon nuclei. The large particle size of the obtained product may be ascribed to the fact that the ionic liquid created a good reaction environment, thus proceeding with the polycondensation reaction. Subsequently, the sulfur content decreased with the reaction time being extended to 120 min, accompanied by a decreased particle size.

XPS spectra of the prepared IL-CDs in Fig. S2 show that there are mainly C, N, O and S elements on the surface of IL-CDs. As seen from the C 1 s spectra in Fig. S3(a), four peaks can be fitted and assigned to CC (∼284.7 eV), C-N/C-S (∼285.5 eV), C-O (∼286.5 eV), CO (∼288.2 eV) [31], which thus confirms the successful incorporation of N, S into the IL-CDs.
In O 1 s spectra (in Fig. S3(b)), there are three peaks, namely CO (∼531.0 eV), COC (∼531.7 eV), OCO (∼532.8 eV) [32].

In Fig. 4 (a), S 2p spectrum shows three characteristic peaks appearing at 163.3, 164.4 and 168.2 eV, assigned to S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2 of the C-S bond and C-SOx groups [33], respectively. The presence of C-SOx is originated from the oxidation of group -SH. The existence of S2− occurs after synthesis for 120 min due to the excessive reaction by the reduction of the sulfur elements.

The N 1 s spectrum shows three peaks (Fig. 4(b)), pyridine-like nitrogen (∼398.2 eV), pyrrole-like nitrogen (∼399.5 eV) [34] and graphite nitrogen (∼401.3 eV) [35]. The relative amount of nitrogen species presented in the IL-CDs samples is shown in Table 1 . Usually, the nitrogen on pyridine provides lone pair electrons to form a direct Fe-N bond perpendicular with the metal surface [36]; and the pyrrole interacts with the metal surface through the aromatic ring to form a π complex, allowing the pyrrole ring to be parallel to the metal surface [37]. Therefore, the adsorption of pyrroles contributes more to corrosion inhibition. From Table 1, we can see that IL-CDs3 have more pyrrole nitrogen, and therefore they may have the best corrosion inhibition performance.

The Nyquist and relevant Bode plots for the carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing each of the four IL-CDs are displayed in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that in the absence and the presence of IL-CDs, the shape of Nyquist and Bode plots of carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution is similar, which indicates that the addition of carbon dots inhibitor won’t change the corrosion mechanism. The depressed semicircles at high frequencies are caused by the surface inhomogeneity; the inductive arc appearing at low frequency is related to the relaxation process of adsorbed species like (SO4 2−)ad and/or (H+)ad onto the steel surface [38]. The radius of the capacitive semicircle increases with the synthesis time of IL-CDs (≤60 min), and decreases after further increasing synthesis time to 120 min. The blank sample CDs prepared without IL assistance show the lowest inhibition ability (Fig. 5(a1)). Therefore, the optimum synthesis time is 60 min; the excessive reaction can decrease the inhibition performance, probably caused by the decrease of S content in IL-CDs4 listed in Table S1. Meanwhile, the radius of the capacitive semicircle increase with the concentration of IL-CDs (Fig. 5(b1)). Therefore, the sample IL-CDs3 prepared at 60 min was selected for subsequent testing and characterization.

From Bode plots of the four IL-CDs in Fig. 5(b2), it can be seen that the impedance modulus increases with the reaction time first and reaches a maximum at 60 min, and then decreases when further increasing reaction time to 120 min. For different concentrations of IL-CDs3, the impedance modulus increases with the concentration of IL-CDs3. The phase angle shows one phase peak when uninhibited by IL-CDs, suggesting a two-time constant. Meanwhile, the frequency range with the maximum phase angle offers the same trends with the impedance modulus in cases of different reaction time and IL-CDs concentrations. The present results reveal the two-time-constant equivalent circuit (Fig. 6 ) is suitable for with and without the IL-CDs solution [39].

The inhibition performance of carbon steel with inhibitors can be evaluated by the low-frequency impedance modulus (Z), which increases with the increment of the concentration of the inhibitor, thus implying that the corrosion of carbon steel is retarded [38].

In the equivalent circuit model, Rs is the solution resistance, Rp is the polarisation resistance, L is the inductance, and RL is the inductive resistance. The presence of inductance L indicates that Fe dissolution of carbon steel can still occur by charge transfer in the presence of IL-CDs. CPE is the constant phase element, which is often used in place of capacitance to obtain more accurate results. CPE is expressed as follows: [40].

For n = 0, CPE is pure resistance (R); n = 1, CPE represents an ideal capacitor (C); n = −1, CPE represents inductance (L), and n = 0.5, CPE is Warburg impedance (W).

where ωmax is the angular frequency at the maximum of the imaginary part of the impedance spectrum, ωmax = 2πfmax .

In Table 2 , Rp increases and Cdl decreases with the increase of the IL-CDs synthesis time up to 60 min. However, Rp decreases and Cdl increases when further increasing synthesis time to 120 min, which indicates the optimum protective film formed by the IL-CDs3 synthesized for 60 min.

In Table 3 , Rp increases while Cdl decreases with increasing IL-CDs concentration, which could be interpreted as the formation of an adsorption layer by IL-CDs at the interface between the electrode and solution [41]. In addition, more concentrations of inhibitors are added, the thicker the covering film might be formed, leading to the decrease of the dielectric constant of the adsorbed layer or the increase of the thickness of the double layer, according to the following equation: [42].

where R0 p and Rp are the polarisation resistance for the steel in the studied solution uninhibited and inhibited, respectively.

In Table 2, the inhibition efficiency of the four IL-CDs at a concentration of 40 mg/L decreases as follows: IL-CDs3 > IL-CDs4 > IL-CDs2 > IL-CDs1 > CDs, with the highest inhibition efficiency (IE) of 97.0%. Compared with the CDs, the IL-CDs can protect the steel surface more effectively, suggesting that IL assistance benefits the carbon dots formation with good inhibition performance.

The effect of the inhibitor concentration on the inhibition efficiency can be seen in Table 3. The inhibition efficiency increases with IL-CDs3 concentration. Compared with corrosion inhibition performance of carbon dots reported in previous publications listed in Table S2, a high inhibition efficiency (97.0%) at a small concentration of IL-CDs3 (40 mg/L) has been obtained in this study, which can be attributed to the advantages of IL-assisted preparation by reducing the agglomeration of CDs demonstrated by TEM images in Fig. 3. Moreover, a shorter preparation time of carbon dots can be obtained in the presence of IL than that reported in the literature [39,44-48].

Different synthesis times and inhibitor concentrations have a significant effect on the corrosion current densities of the inhibitors. For IL-CDs prepared by varying synthetic time, the corrosion current densities of IL-CDs at 40 mg/L decreases (≤60 min) first and then decreases with excess reaction time (120 min). All four IL-CDs show lower corrosion current densities than that of uninhibited solution. The CDs prepared without ionic liquid can also decrease the corrosion current densities, while having the larger corrosion current densities than all IL-CDs. Take IL-CDs3 as an example, the anodic and cathodic current densities decreased with increasing concentrations of IL-CDs. Compared to the blank solution, solution containing IL-CDs, seen from the OCP (in Fig. S4) the maximum corrosion potential (Ecorr ) shift less than ± 85 mV, revealing that all the IL-CDs on the carbon steel surface belongs to mixed-type inhibitors, retarding both anodic and cathodic reaction [43]. In the presence of IL-CDs, the desorption potentials occur in the anodic polarisation region, demonstrating the appearance of desorption of the IL-CDs.

The inhibition efficiency is obtained from the corrosion current density (icorr ) obtained from the polarization data using the following equation [42]:

where η% refers to the inhibition efficiency gained from polarization tests, and i0 corr and icoor represent the corrosion current density of carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 uninhibited and inhibited solution, respectively.

The electrochemical parameters are obtained by calculation using the Tafel extrapolation and listed in Tables 4 and 5
.

As compared with the uninhibited one, icorr in the presence of IL-CDs inhibitors decreases significantly, demonstrating the excellent corrosion inhibition performance of IL-CDs. Moreover, the i0 corr at 40 mg/L increases initially with reaction time, and reaches the maximum at 60 min with a perfect inhibition performance of 96.7%, then decreases with further increased reaction time to 120 min. Table 5 indicates the inhibition performance of IL-CDs increases with IL-CDs concentration, which is consistent with the results of the EIS tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature and exposure time
ce:section.ce:para: Exposure temperature is an essential parameter for corrosion inhibition performance. The EIS results for 40 mg/L IL-CDs3 towards carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 8 , and the obtained electrochemical parameters are shown in Table 6 . It can be seen from Fig. 8 that as temperature increases, the shape of the Nyquist plot is still a double capacitance loop at high to medium frequency and an inductive loop at low frequency, which does not change with temperature. Therefore, the equivalent circuit in Fig. 6 was used for fitting the EIS data. The data in Table 6 shows that the corrosion rate gradually decreases with the increase of temperature, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency changes from 97.0% to 82.3% by increasing temperature from 298 K to 318 K. This can be explained by the enhanced desorption of IL-CDs3 on the steel surface caused by increasing temperature, leading to reduced corrosion inhibition efficiency.

Fig. 9 shows the changes in the inhibition performance of carbon steel exposed to 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing 40 mg/L of IL-CDs3 at 298 K for different exposure time (1, 2, 4, 8 and 12 h). From the shape of the Nyquist plot by changing immersion time from 1 to 4 h, the equivalent circuit in Fig. 6 can be used for fitting the EIS data. However, the impedance arc changes into a single capacitive loop when increasing exposure time from 8 to 12 h, which may correspond to the relaxation process occurring on the surface of the carbon steel and the desorption of the adsorbed species from the steel surface. Therefore, the simple equivalent circuit shown in Fig. S5 is for the EIS data fitting. The results in Table 7 shows that the inhibition performance gradually increases with exposure time, with the highest inhibition performance of 98.6% achieved by steel immersion in solution for 12 h with 40 mg/L IL-CDs3.

For gaining an in-depth analysis of the surface morphology of steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with and without inhibitor, it was immersed at 298 K for 2 h. The SEM test has been carried out, and their images are shown in Fig. 10 . It reveals that the polished steel surface is relatively smooth with some scratches (Fig. 10(a)). However, the steel surface is severely corroded with some products and cracks after immersion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, as illustrated in Fig. 10(b). In the presence of IL-CDs (Fig. 10(c, d, e, and f)), the roughness of the steel surfaces significantly decreases, showing a few products and shallow cracks. Therefore, the IL-CDs can effectively prevent the steel from acid attack.

AFM images of the carbon steel surface with and without 40 mg/L IL-CDs are shown in Fig. 11 . The steel surface has obvious depression with a large average roughness (Ra = 118 nm) after immersion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution (Fig. 11(a)). However, the steel surfaces after inhibition shown in Fig. 11(b, c, d, and e) are smoother. Moreover, the values of the average roughness after the addition of the IL-CDs1, IL-CDs2, IL-CDs3, and IL-CDs4 are 45.1, 37.5, 22.2, 31.6 nm, respectively, which are significantly lower than that of the steel surface uninhibited, indicating that the IL-CDs have a significant inhibitory effect, which is in high consistent with the electrochemical results and SEM analysis.

Experimental data obtained for carbon steel at temperatures of 298 K, 303 K, 308 K, and 318 K were plotted as Arrhenius diagrams, and kinetic parameters were calculated according to the following equation [49,50]:
(6)

where CR is the corrosion rate, Ea is the activation energy calculated from the slope of the straight line in the Arrhenius diagram (Fig. 12 a), P is the prefactor, R is the gas constant with a value of 8.314 (j∙mol−1), T is the thermodynamic Kelvin temperature.

The activation enthalpy (ΔHa ) and the activation entropy (ΔSa ) can be calculated separately by plotting the transition state and combining the slope and intercept obtained in the transition state diagram by the following equation [51]:
(7)

where h is the Plank’s constant, N is the Avogadro’s number. The calculated activation enthalpy and activation entropy are listed in Table 9 .

The activation energy (Ea ) in the presence of inhibitor is higher than in the absence of inhibitor, and increases gradually with IL-CDs3 concentration, indicating more IL-CDs3 inhibitor adsorbed on the steel surface forms a protection film, leading to effective corrosion inhibition [51,52]. Both ΔHa and ΔSa are higher in inhibited solution than the uninhibited one, which is due to the inhibition of the corrosion reaction of carbon steel [53]. ΔHa is positive in all cases, which indicates that the corrosion reaction of carbon steel is endothermic. The activation entropy ΔSa increases gradually with concentration, suggesting that the disorder in the adsorption process rises slowly, and an activated complex is formed on the steel surface [54].

The inhibition mechanism for most inhibitors in acidic media could be due to the adsorption of inhibitors onto the carbon steel surfaces [34]. To get deep insights into the inhibition mechanism of IL-CDs, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm was tested, and it was found to be the best fit to estimate the inhibition mechanism, which was obtained by the following equation [45]:
(8)
C
inh

=
C
inh
+
1
K
ads
where Cinh and Kads denote the inhibitor concentration and the adsorption equilibrium constant, respectively, and θ (θ = η%/100) is the surface coverage.

Fig. 13 shows the plots of C/θ vs. C after fitting PDP results. Take IL-CDs3 as an example, this relationship is approximated by a straight line with a slope of 0.959, an intercept of 1/Kads = 3.02, and a linear correlation coefficient R2 close to 0.999, which gives a calculated Kads of 331.5 L/g.

Based on the known value of Kads , the standard adsorption free energy of the inhibitor, ΔGθ ads can be obtained from the following equation [55]:
(9)
Δ G
ads

= - R T l n
( 1000
K
ads
)
where R is the gas constant with a value of 8.314 (j∙mol−1), T is the thermodynamic Kelvin temperature, and 1000 is the mass concentration of water.

The magnitude of the Kads value is related to the excellent inhibition performance. In most cases, the higher the Kads value, the stronger the adsorption of inhibition onto the steel surface, thus the better inhibition performance [20]. The Kads values of IL-CDs1, IL-CDs2, IL-CDs3, and IL-CDs4 were listed in Fig. 13, and decreases in the following order: IL-CDs3 > IL-CDs4 > IL-CDs2 > IL-CDs1. As a consequence, IL-CDs3 exhibits the best inhibition performance when the reaction time is 60 min.

In general, if the ΔGθ ads ≤ -40 kJ/mol, the adsorption of the inhibitor is chemisorption; if the ΔGθ ads ≥ -20 kJ/mol, the adsorption is physical adsorption, and if it is between −40 kJ/mol and −20 kJ/mol, it can be considered as mixed adsorption (physical adsorption and chemisorption) [36] The calculated standard free energy of adsorption of IL-CDs1, IL-CDs2, IL-CDs3 and IL-CDs4 on the steel surface is −29.2, −30.9, −31.5 and −31.3 kJ/mol, respectively. Therefore, the inhibition performance of IL-CDs can be ascribed to both physical adsorption and chemisorption.

As stated in the literature, the ΔGθ ads indicates that the adsorption process of IL-CDs is mixed adsorption, but the nature of adsorption of corrosion inhibitors, needs to be determined by the ΔHθ ads
[49]. Therefore, we performe the calculation of the ΔHθ ads of IL-CDs3 and the results show that the value of the ΔHθ ads is −22.7 kJ/mol (in Table S5) and the nature of adsorption is physical [49], but the presence of weak chemisorption could not be excluded [56].

With the purpose of investigating the type of adsorption of IL-CDs on the steel surface, XPS was performed by immersion of steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing 40 mg/L IL-CDs3 for 2 h. Fig. 14 (a) presented the testing results and in the C 1 s spectrum, the presence of sp2 C (CC at 284.5 eV), sp3 C (C-N/C-S at 285.5 eV), and CO (288.2 eV) indicates the presence of multiple functional groups on the steel surface [57]. In the N 1 s spectrum in Fig. 14(b), three peaks at 398.8 eV, 399.8 eV, and 401.2 eV are attributed to C-N, N-Fe, and N+H, respectively [32]. The presence of N-Fe indicates that the lone pair of electrons in N forms coordination bonds with iron via chemical adsorption onto the steel surface; As could be observed from Fig. 14(c), S is present as Fe-S (168.1 eV) and SO4 2− (169.4 eV) [32]; In the O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 14(d), three peaks appear at 529.6, 531.0, and 532.8 eV, which are ascribable to the O2−, OH of hydrous iron oxides (such as FeOOH) [34], and OCO, respectively [35]. These results imply that IL-CDs can be adsorbed onto the steel surface via chemical adsorption by complexing with Fe through lone pair electrons provided by N and S, while protonated nitrogen (N+H) is adsorbed onto the steel surface via physical adsorption.

According to some references [2,6,7] and the above surface properties analysis via various characterization methods, a possible inhibition mechanism can be proposed. There are different functional groups riched in the surface of IL-CDs, including pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N species and SOx species etc. The adsorption of IL-CDs onto the steel surface will generate a thin film, which works for the corrosion inhibition against acid attack to the carbon steel.

The IL-CDs3 prepared for 60 min shows the best inhibition performance with an IE of 97%. Among the series of IL-CDs, it is the biggest in average carbon dot sizes (about 117.0 nm), relative high surface N content(11.5 at%) with highest pyrrole-like N contents (62.9%) and lowest pyridine-like N contents (18.0%). Compared with N species, surface S species is relatively low (1.5 ∼ 5.1 at%) and decreases with the synthesis time; surface C-SOx was formed in IL-CDs3, which provides active adsorption centers for corrosion inhibition in acid media, according to our previous work [58].

A schematic illustration of the inhibition mechanism of the carbon steel in tested solution in the absence/presence of IL-CDs has been shown in Fig. 15 . In the CDs adsorption, pyridine-like N species can provide lone pair electrons to form direct Fe-N bond attached to the steel surface, with the pyridine ring nearly perpendicular to the metal surface; Pyrrole ring achieves 6 delocalized π-electrons necessary for aromatic stabilization with two π electrons attributed from nitrogen. Due to the “π-electron excessive” manner of pyrrole ring, it is susceptible to electrophilic attack, forming a π-complex with the iron atom on the steel surface, allowing the parallel adsorption of its ring on the carbon steel. The parallel adsorption mode of pyrrole-like N species improved the coverage of IL-CDs on the carbon steel surface, which finally results in effective inhibition performance [37]. Meanwhile, More sulfonic acid groups are favorable for the chemisorption of IL-CDs on the steel surface, thus promoting the inhibition performance.

In the tested 0.5 M H2SO4 solution for carbon steel, in the anodic reaction, the adsorption and desorption of and SO4 2− ions occur. The positive charge N+H from IL-CDs can interact with the adsorbed species Fe[(H2O)n
S O
4
2 -
]ads to form a protective layer by electrostatical interaction [59]; in the cathodic reaction, the adsorption of hydronium (H3O+) and desorption of hydrogen gas (H2) occurs concurrently. the caction of the IL-CDs, the positive charged IL-CDs, can substitute such as H3O+ to adsorb on the surface of carbon steel [60] and form new adsorption products with Fe, to retard corrosion. Afterwards, IL-CDs are further adsorbed on the metal surface by chemisorption.

A series of N, S-doped CDs have been successfully prepared simply from L-cysteine using solvothermal method assisted with ionic liquid 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide by varying reaction time. Then the IL-CDs were characterized and their corrosion inhibition performance for carbon steel was evaluated in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 298 K, and the effect of temperature, exposure time, and corrosion kinetics parameter was carried out. Finally, the surface analysis such as AFM, XPS and SEM has been taken in the presence/absence of inhibitors to give deep insights into their inhibition mechanism. Based on the electrochemical experiments and surface analysis, the conclusions can be drawn as follows:
(1)
The results of electrochemical tests (EIS and PDP) show that the inhibition efficiency of the IL-CDs increases with the increment of the inhibitor concentration and it can achieve high inhibition efficiency of 93.2–97.0% from EIS and 94.3–96.7% from PDP at low IL-CDs concentration. This was achieved via physical adsorption and chemisorption of carbon dots on the steel surface, which follows the Langmuir isotherm by hindering both the metal dissolution on the anode and hydrogen evolution reaction on the cathode.
(2)
The carbon dots prepared for 60 min shows the best inhibition performance with an inhibition efficiency of 97.0% (EIS), which is probably attributed to the biggest average carbon dot sizes (about 117.0 nm), rich N content(11.5 at%) with the highest pyrrole-like N contents (62.9%) and lowest pyridine-like N contents (18.0%), and increased surface C-SOx functional group.
(3)
Effective inhibition can be observed by SEM, and AFM tests. XPS analysis indicates that the pyrrole-like N species and sulfonate group C-SOx contributes dominantly to good inhibition performance. The possible parallel adsorption modes of pyrrole-like N species may function as a crucial contributor to the excellent inhibition performance.
(4)
The IL-assisted solvothermal preparation of carbon dots provides a new general method, which lowers the preparation time and reduce the aggerragation of CDs to form uniform carbon dots. Due to the versatile family of ionic liquids, this method is promising for designing new corrosion inhibitors.
